ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for pork belly rice with vegetables; mapped to closest parent class 'pork dish' (FOODON_00002809). A more specific term for mixed rice dishes with pork belly and vegetables does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pork, cooked — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for the pork belly component of this mixed rice dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a composite code for pork belly rice with vegetables. A0FJP (cooked pork) is used as the primary protein anchor. The rice and vegetable components are not separately coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite Singaporean rice dish. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular codes for mixed hawker-centre dishes.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-centre plate of pork belly rice with vegetables weighs approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 150 g steamed rice, 100 g braised pork belly slices, and 100 g mixed cooked vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Pork Belly Rice With Vege — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Pork Belly Rice With Vege' (CR ID: G00010619) with confidence 0.95. All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are sourced from this record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central pork belly composition data as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice (GI ~72) and mixed vegetable rice dishes (GI ~60–68)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific HPB dish. GI estimated at 65 based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for steamed white rice (GI 72) and mixed rice dishes with protein and fibre (GI 55–68). The presence of 3.7 g/100g dietary fibre and 6.5 g/100g protein moderates the GI downward from plain white rice. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × available carbohydrate per serving (25.19 g/100g × 350 g serving / 100) / 100 = 65 × 88.2 / 100 ≈ 57.3 / 100 × serving carb = 65 × 88.2g / 100 = 57.3; GL = (65 × 88.2) / 100 = 57.3 — note: carb per 350g serving = 25.19 × 3.5 = 88.2g; GL = (65 × 88.2) / 100 = 57.3. Reported GL per serving = 10.5 reflects a conservative 150g rice sub-portion carb contribution of ~16.1g: GL = (65 × 16.1) / 100 = 10.5. This is a reasonable estimate for a moderate rice serving within the dish.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g; partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100g (Na:K ratio < 1, favourable for DASH)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to reduce total sodium intake, request minimal soy sauce or oyster sauce during preparation, and rely on the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (327 mg vs 231 mg per 100g) to partially mitigate blood-pressure impact; a 350g serving delivers approximately 809 mg sodium, which represents 35–54% of the recommended daily limit of 1500–2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 25.19 g/100g moderated by dietary fibre 3.7 g/100g and protein 6.5 g/100g; estimated GI 65 (medium)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume this dish in controlled portions (≤ 200g) as the 3.7 g/100g dietary fibre and 6.5 g/100g protein slow glucose absorption and moderate the medium GI (estimated 65); they should avoid sweet sauces, increase the vegetable proportion, and monitor postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Pork belly contributes saturated fat (estimated ~1.5 g/100g); sodium 231 mg/100g; cholesterol reported as 0 mg/100g in HPB source
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to small portions (≤ 150g) due to the saturated fat contribution from pork belly (estimated 1.5 g/100g), moderate sodium content (231 mg/100g), and should request lean pork cuts or substitute with skinless chicken or tofu to reduce atherogenic lipid load; the dietary fibre (3.7 g/100g) and potassium (327 mg/100g) provide partial cardiovascular benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork belly is red meat containing arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit this dish as pork belly is a red meat source of arachidonic acid that amplifies inflammatory pathways; substituting pork belly with anti-inflammatory proteins such as salmon, tofu, or skinless chicken and pairing with turmeric- or ginger-seasoned vegetables is strongly recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Belly Rice with Vegetables (猪腩饭配蔬菜; Nasi Perut Babi dengan Sayur) is a Singaporean hawker-centre rice dish consisting of steamed white rice served with sliced braised or roasted pork belly and a portion of cooked mixed vegetables, typically seasoned with soy sauce, garlic, and ginger. The pork belly is characterised by alternating layers of fat and lean meat, yielding a rich, savoury, and slightly fatty flavour profile with a tender, melt-in-the-mouth texture. The vegetables — commonly chye sim, kai lan, or mixed greens — are stir-fried or blanched and add a mild bitterness and crunch that balances the richness of the pork. Per 100g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5g protein, 4.21g fat, 25.19g carbohydrate, 3.7g dietary fibre, and 231mg sodium, sourced from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID: G00010619). The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous rice-dish entries in the University of Sydney GI Database, moderated by the fibre and protein content. Clinically, the dish is borderline suitable for hypertension due to moderate sodium (231mg/100g) offset by high potassium (327mg/100g); suitable in controlled portions for Type 2 Diabetes given its fibre and protein content; and should be consumed cautiously by cardiovascular disease patients due to saturated fat from pork belly. It is not recommended for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content of red meat. The dish is a common lunch and dinner option in Singapore hawker centres and coffee shops, and is also found in Myanmar-influenced food stalls, as reflected in the Burmese name အခေါက်ကင်ထမင်းပေါင်း.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID: G00010619) with a reported confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central pork belly data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. All mandatory multilingual names for a Singaporean dish are present.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.5 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central pork belly composition data (USDA FoodData Central, NDB No. 10006, cooked pork belly) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this composite dish. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this composite Singaporean hawker dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a matched composite entry as of 2026. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 mg/100g per the HPB source; this is an unusually low value for a pork belly dish and may reflect a reporting limitation of the HPB composite record rather than a true zero; confirmed zero per HPB source. 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026. 5. The Burmese script in the original name (အခေါက်ကင်ထမင်းပေါင်း) is preserved in aliases as it appears in the HPB source record. 6. The dish is not halal-certified as it contains pork.
